coredata.dc:title: The preparation and properties of carbon-coated fibers as solid-phase microextraction adsorbents for the determination of triacetone triperoxide in soil samples with fast and sensitive analysis using ion mobility spectrometry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Derikvand, Ahmadreza
Maddah, Bozorgmehr
Asadi, Sajad
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-coated fibers include carbon activated/polydimethylsiloxane (CA/PDMS), graphene/ polydimethylsiloxane (Gr/PDMS), graphene quantum dots/ polydimethylsiloxane (GQD/PDMS), carbon nano-onions/polydimethylsiloxane (CNO/PDMS) and MXene/polydimethylsiloxane (MXene/PDMS) were prepared by sol–gel method, characterized by SEM, EDX and FT-IR techniques and were used by the headspace solid-phase microextraction coupled with ion mobility spectrometry (HS-SPME-IMS) method for extraction and determination of triacetone triperoxide (TATP). According to the results, MXene/PDMS was selected as the best sorbent. A response surface methodology based on a Box Behnken design (BBD) method was used to optimize critical experimental variables. Under optimal extraction conditions, the calibration graph was linear in the 50 to 1000 ng mL−1 (R2 > 0.998). Also, the detection limit and the relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 6) were 15.1 ng mL−1 and 2.68 %, respectively. Finally, the HS-SPME-IMS method was successfully used to analyze TATP in contaminated soil samples. To check the matrix effect and the accuracy of the process, the analyte addition technique was applied (added: 50–1000 ng mL−1; determined: 48–1015 ng mL−1). The desired relative recoveries were obtained in the range of 95.3 to 104.1 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Box Behnken design
Carbon-coated fibers
Explosives
MXene/polydimethylsiloxane
Sol-gel method
Triacetone triperoxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface modification and fiber substrate activation
Fabrication of fiber coating by sol–gel method
Aging, drying and preparation of fabricated fibers
Characterization of the sorbents and TATP
Comparative of the sorbents extraction efficiency
Optimization of the extraction conditions using BBD
Comparison of the MXene/PDMS fiber with commercial fibers and the HS-SPME-IMS method with other methods
Investigation of the effect of vacuum on extraction efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrographs of the MXene/PDMS SPME sorbent at different magnifications, (a) 20.8 µm, (b) 8.30 µm.
Comparison of the amount of extraction (n = 3) by different carbon-based SPME fibers for the extraction of TATP.
Response surface plots for TATP, (a) extraction time versus moisture, (b) extraction temperature versus moisture, (c) extraction temperature versus extraction time.
Ion mobility spectra of TATP sampling of soil samples by HS-SPME-IMS method, (a) before spike, (b) after spike.
Sampling steps by LP-HS-SPME setup (top path) and conventional HS-SPME (down path).
Analytical figures of merits of the HS-SPME-IMS method for model solid samples.
HS-SPME-IMS analysis of TATP in real samples.

head.ce:title: The preparation and properties of carbon-coated fibers as solid-phase microextraction adsorbents for the determination of triacetone triperoxide in soil samples with fast and sensitive analysis using ion mobility spectrometry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Imam Hossein Comprehensive University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Imam Hossein Comprehensive University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-coated fibers include carbon activated/polydimethylsiloxane (CA/PDMS), graphene/ polydimethylsiloxane (Gr/PDMS), graphene quantum dots/ polydimethylsiloxane (GQD/PDMS), carbon nano-onions/polydimethylsiloxane (CNO/PDMS) and MXene/polydimethylsiloxane (MXene/PDMS) were prepared by sol–gel method, characterized by SEM, EDX and FT-IR techniques and were used by the headspace solid-phase microextraction coupled with ion mobility spectrometry (HS-SPME-IMS) method for extraction and determination of triacetone triperoxide (TATP). According to the results, MXene/PDMS was selected as the best sorbent. A response surface methodology based on a Box Behnken design (BBD) method was used to optimize critical experimental variables. Under optimal extraction conditions, the calibration graph was linear in the 50 to 1000 ng mL−1 (R2 > 0.998). Also, the detection limit and the relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 6) were 15.1 ng mL−1 and 2.68 %, respectively. Finally, the HS-SPME-IMS method was successfully used to analyze TATP in contaminated soil samples. To check the matrix effect and the accuracy of the process, the analyte addition technique was applied (added: 50–1000 ng mL−1; determined: 48–1015 ng mL−1). The desired relative recoveries were obtained in the range of 95.3 to 104.1 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The significant increase in the use of explosives in terrorist acts in recent years and the destructive effects of these substances on biological systems in humans and animals have turned them into a global threat [1,2]. It is possible to use readily available materials, such as fertilizers and industrial chemicals containing stable oxidizers and other less stable compounds, to prepare of homemade explosives.

Triacetone triperoxide (TATP) was first discovered and made by Wolfenstein in 1895, which is a material with high sensitivity to friction, shock and impact. The ease of making this combination of the available household and industrial products, the easy use and explosive power of this substance has led to its use in terrorist operations [3]. Some of the TATP physical and chemical properties are shown in Table S1 in the electronic supplementary information (ESI).

Based on this, the development of new methods and technologies for sampling, pre-concentration, faster, more sensitive and more accurate identification of these substances has become increasingly important [4,5]. Analytical methods capable of detecting explosive molecules in low concentrations and the gas or vapor phase have attracted the attention of scientists. All liquid and solid explosive compounds produce a certain amount of vapor, which depends exponentially on the temperature [6].

Solid phase microextraction (SPME) is an analytical technique that can evaluate volatile and semi-volatile compounds in the vapor or liquid phase of solid and fluid materials. In this solvent-free technique, all the steps of sampling, sample preparation, separation and concentration are integrated in one step. In addition, SPME has other advantages, such as automation and connection to various analytical devices such as GC and HPLC, non-disposability of the extractive phase, high sensitivity and suitable linear range, compatibility with the environment, etc. This technique was used in various fields such as pharmaceutical analysis, food, medicine, etc. [7,8].

The best and most common mode of SPME is sampling from the headspace [9,10]. Where the analytes are exposed to the fiber after separating from the matrix and migrating to the headspace, without any connection between the fiber and the matrix. In this method, transferring analytes from the sample to the headspace is considered the speed-determining step [11]. To shorten the equilibration time, strategies such as stirring, sonication, microwave radiation and heating the sample matrix have been proposed. Also, reducing the pressure of the headspace is one of these strategies [12–16].

SPME fibers are commercially available with various coatings. The use of SPME for the extraction and enrichment of TATP based on commercial fibers has been reported [17,18]. However, new SPME coating materials with high extraction capacity, high selectivity, cheaper and more excellent stability are developing. The physicochemical properties of the sorbent are the most critical factors affecting the efficiency and selectivity of the SPME analysis method [19,20]. Carbon fibers have been widely used in separation and purification processes due to their unique properties such as high elastic modulus, strong conductivity, very porous structure, high absorption capacity, etc. The diverse applications of these materials in various fields such as nanocomposites, sensors, etc. have led to the creation of different morphologies of them. Carbon fibers are unique materials that can adsorb many substances [21–23]. Therefore, in this research, carbon materials including graphene, graphene quantum dot, activated carbon, carbon nano-onions, and MXene were used to prepare SPME fiber adsorbents.

Detection of TATP is known by ion mobility spectrometry (IMS) [3,24,25]. IMS is one of the most well-known and proven technologies for detecting explosives. This technique is based on ion separation from the gas phase under atmospheric pressure which can be used in two different modes, positive or negative. IMS has advantages like low detection limit for single-component samples, short analysis time, no need for bulky vacuum systems, and the ability to identify explosives in the field [26].

Experiment design techniques such as Box Behnken design (BBD) of response surface methodology (RSM) provide optimization of the parameters of a method in a short time by reducing the number of experiments. The interaction between factors is also investigated with these techniques. These methods help predict future results, improve efficiency, and correct errors [27,28].

In this research, five types of carbon fibers were made and their efficiency was investigated to extract and determine TATP using the HS-SPME-IMS method. According to our best search, until now, in a separate research, the preparation of different types of carbon fibers by sol–gel method as SPME fiberd coating and comparing their performance with each other and with commercial fibers has not been done. For the first time, MXene/PDMS composite fiber coating was introduced, which had better performance than commercial fibers. Also, the HS-SPME-IMS method is a sensitive, fast and cheap method compared to other methods that have been reported for TATP analysis such as GC–MS and HPLC-FT-IR. The effect of the vacuum on TATP extraction efficiency was investigated using LP-HS-SPME-IMS method with the help of a portable device to create vacuum.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), graphite, activated carbon, potassium permanganate and citric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Methanol, hydrofuran, acetone, hydrogen peroxide, sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, hydrochloric acid, trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), and dichloromethane solvents were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) with analytical purity and used without any additional purification. Max phase and diamond nanoparticles were obtained from Nano Bazaar (Tehran, Iran). A standard stock solution (1000 ppm) of TATP was prepared and kept in a fridge. Working standard solutions were prepared by diluting the stock solution weekly. Standard sand was purchased from the National Water Research Institute of Canada (NWRI, Burlington, Canada) and used as a matrix to optimize the method. Stainless steel wire with a thickness of 0.07 mm was obtained from Vita Needle (MA, USA) and was used as the SPME fiber substrate.

Correct and accurate weighing of materials, soil samples and commercial sand was done using a digital scale precisa model XB-220A (Switzerland). To make uniform solutions, the SONOREX DIGITEC model ultrasonic device made in Germany was used. PerkinElmer- Spectrum 100 spectrometer (Waltham, Massachusetts) was used to record the Fourier transform infrared spectra. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) techniques were performed using Tescan-MIRA3 (Brno, Czech Republic). SEM was used to study the surface morphology, and EDX was applied to investigate the chemical composition of the sorbents. To separate, quantify and detect the analyte, a CD-IMS model 1400 manufactured by Teif Azmon Spadana Company (Isfahan University of Technology, Iran) with a corona discharge (CD) ionization source was used. The conditions of the IMS used in this study are given in Table S2 in the ESI. Minitab statistical software (Version 16, Microsoft Corporation, USA) was used to design and process the experimental data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification and fiber substrate activation
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare strong and durable fibers, the surface of the stainless-steel substrate was activated. In this regard, the stainless-steel wires were cut into 7.0 cm pieces, washed with water and methanol, and then dried. Subsequently, these parts were immersed in 2.0 M sulfuric acid solution for 5.0 min, washed with water, and dried. After that, their surface was oxidized by exposure to air and high temperature (350 ◦C) for 2 hr in a furnace. This procedure forms a thin layer of oxide mainly composed of Fe2O3 and Cr2O3 by taking out surface carbon substances [29]. Then, to activate the superficial hydroxyl groups in the oxide layer, the wires were immersed in 1.0 M sodium hydroxide solution for 1 hr. In the last step, they were washed with HCl (0.1 M) and water and dried at room temperature.

In this research, graphene was obtained through the reduction of graphene oxide by hydrothermal method. Graphene oxide was synthesized by the improved Hammers method. For this purpose, first, 1.0 g of graphite was dispersed in a solution consisting of 100 mL of sulfuric acid and 13 mL of phosphoric acid under ultrasonic waves for 1 hr. In the next step, 6.0 g of potassium permanganate was added to the solution for 30 min and stirred at 50 °C for 24 hr. Then, the reaction container was transferred to the ice bath, 150 and 3.0 mL of water and hydrogen peroxide were added to it, respectively, until the solution turned yellow. After placing the solution in a centrifuge and separating the sediment, the resulting sediment was washed three times with 5 % hydrochloric acid, then five times with distilled water and finally dried in an oven at 60 °C [30]. To reduce the graphene oxide obtained up to this step, by hydrothermal method, at the beginning, 25 mg of graphene oxide was dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water using ultrasonic waves and transferred to an autoclave. The autoclave was placed in the oven at the temperature of 180 °C for 6 hr. In the end, the autoclave was taken out from the oven, and after its temperature decreased, the black deposit was separated from the bottom of the container [31].

4.0 g of citric acid was transferred to a 25 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C. After 5 min, the substance became a liquid and its color changed from colorless to light yellow. Heating continued for another 25 min, which resulted in the color of the liquid changing from light yellow to orange. This implies the formation of graphene quantum dots. In the next step, to neutralize the pH of the solution, first 10 mL of distilled water and then 0.1 M of sodium hydroxide solution were added drop by drop to the experimental container. Eventually, the solution was poured into the watch glass and was released at room temperature to dry [32].

For the synthesis of this material, first, 1.0 g of Ti2AlC MAX phase was slowly added to 10 mL of HF (10 % w/w) and kept for 24 hr under the temperature of 35 °C and stirring at a speed of 150 rpm. After the reaction, the multilayers of Ti2C were carefully washed with deionized water until the pH of the filtrate reached 7.0. The Ti2C nanosheets were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min and then dried at 80 °C for 1 hr [33].

CNOs nanoparticles were synthesized based on the method proposed by Kuzenstov et al. For this purpose, diamond nanoparticles were placed in a furnace under an argon atmosphere and at 1150 °C for 1 hr. Then, they were placed in a conventional furnace at 400 °C for 1 h to remove amorphous carbon after slow cooling. The obtained products were named CNOs [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fiber coating by sol–gel method
ce:section.ce:para: 20.0 mg of carbon material was added to the 2 mL container containing 120 μL of TEOS, 120 μL of PDMS, and 120 μL of dichloromethane and then sonicated for 15 min. In the next step, 90 μL of TFA was added to the container and subjected to ultrasonic waves for another half hour. After this period, the test container was removed from the ultrasonic device, and the solution was left for 5 min [35]. In the end, 1.0 cm of stainless-steel wire was inserted into the solution, and after 1 min, it was removed from the solution at a low speed. To achieve the desired thickness for the fiber, this work was repeated ten times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aging, drying and preparation of fabricated fibers
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the capillary forces caused by the evaporation of the solvent during the drying step, the coatings prepared by the sol–gel method are associated with shrinkage and cracking, which is undesirable. To reduce or eliminate cracking of the fiber coating, measures such as aging, drying, preparation, and cleaning of the fiber have been considered. In the aging step, the fibers were left at room temperature for 12 hr to complete the compression reactions that take place as a function of time and cause more growth of the gel and its thickness, as well as the creation of more transverse connections. Then, the fibers were washed with water and methanol [36]. In drying and preparing the fibers, to remove the unreacted compounds from the cell solution that remain inside the gel cavities, the prepared fibers are first heated for 1 hr at 100 °C in the oven. After that, they were placed in the injection port of the gas chromatography device at 200 °C for one another hour.

3.0 mmol of acetone and 1.5 mmol of hydrogen peroxide 30 % were combined in a sample vial and cooled to 0 °C in an ice bath. Then, 0.015 mmol of 2 M sulfuric acid was added to the solution as a catalyst. The sample vial was kept at room temperature for 24 hr. After 24 hr, the white residue of TATP was formed and filtered under vacuum. Eventually, the precipitate was recrystallized three times by hot methanol [37].

To apply vacuum and withdraw the air from the headspace, the system designed by Ghiasvand and his colleagues was used. The design of this system is such that its central part includes two nested glass vials, one of which is used as a sample vial and the other as a vacuum chamber. The vacuum chamber (outer container) has a silicone stopper cap and a side outlet for the vacuum gauge. A conical GC septum was incorporated into the cap for entering the needles. The sample vial (inner container), a 10 mL glass vial with a silicone stopper cap, was placed inside the vacuum chamber. Evacuation was carried out by withdrawing the air using a 50-mL Luer-Lock plastic syringe whose needle passed through the septum of the vacuum chamber. After evacuation, the cap of the sample vial was removed by the wire connected to it, making a pathway for the release of analytes in the vacuum chamber. Eventually, the needle of the SPME fiber holder was passed through the septum to adsorb the analytes, and the fiber was exposed to the headspace of the sample [9].

In order to extraction of the analyte by the HS-SPME method, the amount of 2.5 g of commercial sand was transferred into the 20 mL sample vial. After that, 10 µl of TATP solution (100 ppm) was spiked to make the analyte concentration in commercial sand 1.0 ppm, and then 10 µl of distilled water was added to it and the lid of the vial was closed. The vial was shaken vigorously for 2 min to mix the contents thoroughly. After that, the sample vial was transferred into the water bath at the temperature of 50 °C, and the SPME fiber was exposed to the sample's headspace. After 10 min, the fiber was withdrawn from the headspace and injected into the IMS injection port for analytes analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the sorbents and TATP
ce:section.ce:para: To study the structure of the functional groups of the synthesized adsorbents, their FT-IR spectra were recorded and analyzed separately. For this purpose, the spectrum of each carbon material was studied individually and then compared with the spectrum of fiber made by the sol–gel method to fully ensure the formation of the composite, which is discussed in detail in the ESI.

In addition, to ensure the correct synthesis of TATP, its FT-IR spectrum was recorded (Fig. S6 in the ESI) and compared with the FT-IR spectrum of this material in the articles. The peaks of 778, 841, and 889 cm−1 are allocated to the O-O stretching bond. Also, the peaks at 1185, 1277 and 1235 cm−1 belong to three weak to intense O–C–O and H3C–C–CH3 asymmetric stretch, H3C–C–O symmetric stretch and O–C–O asymmetric stretch, respectively. The peaks of the two regions 2949 and 3006 cm−1 also indicate the asymmetric stretching of CH3. A comparison of the synthesized TATP spectrum with the peaks in the articles showed that the spectra were in perfect agreement [38,39].

To investigate the surface morphology of the prepared fibers, SEM micrographs were recorded at different magnifications, which are shown in Fig. 1 (related to MXene/PDMS fiber) and Fig. S7 in the ESI (related to other fibers). As seen from the SEM images, the sorbents are uniformly coated, and their layer-by-layer structure provides highly porous surfaces, which leads to high adsorption capacity and high extraction efficiency. Elemental mapping obtained from EDX-SEM analysis was done to determine the percentage of different elements in the synthesized sorbents. The results showed that the elements are distributed uniformly and without accumulating on the fibers coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative of the sorbents extraction efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, in this research, five sorbents including CA/PDMS, Gr/PDMS, GQD/PDMS, CNO/PDMS and MXene/PDMS were prepared, characterized and used for TATP analysis. In order to compare the extraction efficiency of sorbents and choose the best sorbent, all of them were tested under the same conditions mentioned in section 2.12. According to the obtained results, which are shown in Fig. 2 , MXene/PDMS was chosen as the best sorbent. High thermal stability, short preparation time and low memory effect are the most essential features of MXene/PDMS coated fiber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the extraction conditions using BBD
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the extraction conditions and investigate the interaction between the variables, a response surface method based on the BBD was used. The BBD model was designed by applying three variables including extraction time “t”, extraction temperature “T” and amount of water “M”, that the upper and lower levels for each variable are shown in Table S3. The BBD design matrix resulted in 15 experimental runs, which are presented in Table S4. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to evaluate the quality of the proposed RSM model. The design allowed the response to be modeled by a quadratic polynomial fit. The results show (Table 1 ) that the effect of three independent variables (T, t and M) is significant at the 95 % confidence level (that is, p < 0.05). The quality of fit of the quadratic polynomial model was displayed by the R-squared (R2) and P-value. The proposed model was significant with exceptionally high value of R2 (0.9921) and the P-value for the model is lower than 0.05 (0.000). The binary interactions between the independent variables were not significant (the P-value values ​​for them were more outstanding than 0.05).

As shown in Fig. 3 , three-dimensional response surface plots were created to obtain optimal values and interactions of important variables. Fig. S8 in the ESI shows the effect of each investigated factor separately. The graph in Fig. S8a relates to the impact of temperature on TATP extraction efficiency. As it is evident in this graph, with the increase of temperature up to 56 °C, the amount of analyte extraction increases with a sharp slope and after that point it decreases with a relatively more minor slope. Increasing the sample temperature, led to increases the diffusion coefficient of the analytes and shortens the equilibration time. This led to the rise in the extraction speed or mass transfer speed to the fiber coating, this process happens up to an optimal temperature. Further increase in the temperature of the sample leads to a significant decrease in the fiber/headspace distribution constant and the amount of analyte adsorbed on the fiber decreases. Also, since the adsorption of the analyte on the fiber coating is physical, these weak physical connections of the analyte molecules are broken at higher extraction temperatures. In addition, the adsorption of compounds on the fiber is an exothermic process, and high temperature reduces the distribution coefficients of the compounds between the headspace and the fiber and reduces the tendency of the fiber coating to absorb the analyte [40–42].

Extraction time is another important influencing factor, which can be seen in Fig. S8b. According to the graph, it can be said that TATP extraction efficiency increases with a relatively high slope at first. After a particular time, the amount of extraction decreases with a gentle slope. HS-SPME is an equilibrium-based method. Extraction equilibrium was reached in 19 min for the TATP. The graph related to the effect of moisture is shown in the Fig. S8c. Based on this graph, the amount of extracted analyte first increases with a low slope and after reaching a certain amount of moisture (14.5 µL), it decreases with a gentle slope. Adding polar solvents, including water, to the sample matrix, increases the extraction efficiency. Due to the presence of active adsorption sites in the solid sample matrix and the low energy interactions of the target analyte with these sites, adding water to this sample accelerates the release of the analyte. During this process, the sample matrix and solvent compete for analyte adsorption. Therefore, a part of the analyte is released from the sample matrix [43,44]. On the other hand, by increasing the amount of water in volumes higher than the optimal, due to the increase in water vapor pressure in the upper space and as a result, the evaporated water molecules prevent the adsorption of the analyte by the fiber coating, so the extraction efficiency decreases. As a result, the optimal values for extraction temperature, extraction time and sample moisture were determined as 56 °C, 19 min and 14.5 µL respectively.

To investigate the practical application of the HS-SPME-IMS technique, the analytical figures of the method's merit, including the linear dynamic range (LDR), Limit of quantification (LOQ), limit of detection (LOD) and relative standard deviation (RSD), for the determination of TATP in soil matrix under optimal conditions, were evaluated. LDR was in the range of 50 to 1000 ppb, with a linear coefficient of determination above 0.99. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was obtained from the lowest value of the linear limit, which was 50 ppb. The LOD, calculated as three times the standard deviation of the blank (3Sb), was obtained to be 15.1 ppb using a graphical approach [45]. Also, the RSD value of the method for six repeated measurements was 2.68 %. The results are summarized in Table 2 .

In order to evaluate the application and performance of the HS-SPME-IMS method in determination of TATP in real samples, two soil samples were collected from two different places (Tehran, Iran). Then they experimented by the technique mentioned in section 2.12 under optimal conditions. For this purpose, first, to evaluate the initial concentration of the analyte in the actual samples, the corresponding ion mobility spectra were recorded without spiking of the analyte to the soil samples. In the next step, to evaluate matrix effects, standard solutions at three different concentrations were spiked into the soil samples until to make the analyte concentration in the samples reaches three values close to the lower, middle and upper limit of LDR (so-called LOQ, MOQ and HOQ), respectively. Then the mentioned samples were analyzed using HS-SPME-IMS method. The ion mobility spectra of one of the samples are provided in Fig. 4 .

All non-spiked and spiked samples were analyzed six times and RSD values were calculated. Also, relative recovery values (RRs %) were calculated using the following equation:
(1)
RR % =
C
found
-
C
real
C
added
In this formula, Cfound, Creal and Cadd are, respectively, the determined concentration of TATP after addition a particular amount of the standard solution to the real sample, the concentration of TATP in the real sample and a certain concentration of the standard solution added to the real sample. All the results are given in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the MXene/PDMS fiber with commercial fibers and the HS-SPME-IMS method with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the extraction efficiency of MXene/PDMS sorbent in TATP adsorption, this sorbent was compared with commercial sorbents such as Polydimethylsiloxane/Divinylbenzene (PDMS/DVB), Carboxen/Polydimethylsiloxane (CAR/PDMS) and polyacrylate (PA) under optimal conditions. The obtained results show the higher extraction efficiency of the MXene/PDMS sorbent than the other three commercial adsorbents with different polarities for the extraction of the TATP (Fig. S9). In addition, unlike commercial sorbents, this sorbent is not fragile, it is cheap, and it has a high adsorption capacity due to the high surface-to-volume ratio due to the porous structure and the presence of active sites.

To further evaluate the HS-SPME-IMS method, its analytical figures of merit were compared with some similar literature methods for TATP analysis (Table S5). As can be seen, the mentioned method has a lower LOQ than other methods. In addition, the RSD of this method is lower or comparable to previously reported methods. Also, its LOD is lower than most of the mentioned methods. Finally, it can be said that this method has acceptable accuracy and is considered a susceptible technique for pre-concentration and determination of trace amounts of the desired analyte in real samples.

Since the mobility of an ion in a particular gas and a specific electric field is considered a unique quantity, it can be used to identify the ion species. The reduced ion mobility is calculated from the following equation:
(2)
K 0
( unknown )
=
K 0
( standard )
×
t d
( standard )
t d
( unknown )
In this formula, K0 is the reduced ion mobility with unit (cm2 V−1 s−1) and td is the drift time. In this study, nicotinamide was used as an external standard to calibrate the reduced ion mobility scale. Accordingly, the reduced mobility value for the TATP was 1.57 cm2 V−1 s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the effect of vacuum on extraction efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of the vacuum on extraction efficiency, TATP was determined under optimal conditions, once by the HS-SPME-IMS method and another time by the LP-HS-SPME-IMS method (Fig. 5 ). Based on the results, as can be seen in Fig. S10, pressure reduction has a significant effect on TATP extraction efficiency. By applying a vacuum and reducing pressure, the desired analyte is quickly separated from the sample matrix and transferred to the headspace. In addition, because in this method the air is evacuated and consequently there is no air molecule in the headspace, the analyte is more easily and quickly adsorbed into the SPME fiber.

The HS-SPME-IMS method was investigated for the sampling of TATP in solid and complex matrices. For this purpose, five carbon-coated fibers, including CA/PDMS, Gr/PDMS, GQD/PDMS, CNO/PDMS and MXene/PDMS were prepared by sol–gel method and their extraction efficiency was investigated. According to the results, MXene/PDMS fiber was chosen as the best fiber. Compared to common commercial SPME fibers, MXene/PDMS fiber is cheaper and has better performance. Important experimental variables were optimized using the response surface methodology based on a BBD model. The effect of vacuum on extraction efficiency was investigated using the LP-HS-SPME-IMS technique, and the results showed that vacuum has a significant effect on extraction efficiency. The applicability and reliability of the HS-SPME-IMS method were confirmed by its successful application in the analysis of contaminated soil samples. The proposed method indicated the good precision (2.68 %), good linearity (R2 > 0.998), favorable relative recovery (95.3 to 104.1 %) and low detection limit (15.1 ng mL−1). Compared to similar published methods, this method has lower LOQ and lower or comparable RSD. Also, its LOD is lower than many of these methods.

Ahmadreza Derikvand: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bozorgmehr Maddah: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Sajad Asadi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank Research Council of Imam Hossein University for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
